perturbation.original_text: All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party and, upon request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party shall promptly return to the disclosing Party all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, or any part or reproduction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: All CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION will remain the property of the disclosing Party and, upon request of the disclosing Party, the receiving Party shall return to the disclosing Party all CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION, or any part or reproduction thereof, within 90 days of the request.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe to 'within 90 days' introduces a potential conflict. If the disclosing party needs the information back immediately to comply with a legal request (e.g., subpoena, audit), the 90-day delay could put the receiving party in breach of contract and also potentially violate a legal obligation to provide information promptly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45 (Subpoena)
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 45 requires compliance with subpoenas, and a 90-day delay in returning confidential information could prevent a party from fulfilling their legal obligation to respond to a subpoena in a timely manner, leading to potential sanctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a 90-day timeframe for returning confidential information, which contradicts the requirement for timely compliance with subpoenas under FRCP Rule 45. The original contract mandated prompt return upon request. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing FRCP Rule 45, outlines the requirements of a subpoena, including the production of documents. The 90-day delay directly undermines the subpoena's ability to compel immediate document production, as the receiving party could delay compliance, potentially leading to legal sanctions as per the Law Explanation. The inconsistency between the contract's 90-day clause and the law's demand for timely compliance creates a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire five (5) years from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each and every Party, and each employee and officer of each Party Under this Agreement will expire one (1) year from the termination of the JRA.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could violate trade secret protection laws. If the confidential information legitimately requires more than one year to lose its competitive edge, the one-year restriction is insufficient to protect the disclosing party's trade secrets. It is especially problematic if the JRA relates to patentable information, where the information might give a competitor an advantage for more than a year. This is contextual and depends on the exact information covered.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Trade Secret Law, based on the Restatement of Torts § 757, Comment b.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secrets require a reasonable period of protection to maintain their value. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable if the information retains commercial value beyond that timeframe. The reasonableness is determined by looking at the specific trade secret at issue.

Please review this information before proceeding. Terms of use Your use of this service is subject to the LexisNexis� Terms & Conditions. You must accept these Terms & Conditions to use the service. Please also review the Privacy & Cookies policy
perturbation.justification: The original contract provided a five-year confidentiality period, which the changed text reduces to one year. The Law Explanation states that trade secrets require a 'reasonable period of protection to maintain their value' and that a one-year period 'may be deemed unreasonable if the information retains commercial value beyond that timeframe.' The Perturbation Explanation supports this, noting that a one-year restriction may be insufficient to protect trade secrets and that it's 'especially problematic if the JRA relates to patentable information.' This creates a contradiction because the modified text reduces the protection period to a potentially unreasonable timeframe, undermining the legal protection afforded to trade secrets that require longer than one year to lose their value. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a LexisNexis sign-in page and therefore unhelpful, the inconsistency directly impacts the enforceable protection of trade secrets, which is a legally recognized right. This situation falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements for trade secret protection, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is subject to the laws (excluding conflicts rules) of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is subject to the laws (excluding conflicts rules) of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law to Delaware could create issues if the JRA (Joint Research Agreement) work is primarily performed and the parties are located in New York. While parties generally can choose a governing law, it must bear a reasonable relationship to the agreement. If there's no connection to Delaware, a court may refuse to enforce that choice, applying New York law instead, leading to uncertainty and potential disputes over which state's laws apply.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Conflicts of Law regarding Choice of Law Provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 dictates that the law chosen by the parties will be applied unless there is no substantial relationship to the chosen state or application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from New York to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this is problematic because it could lead to uncertainty if the agreement has no reasonable relationship to Delaware, as a court might refuse to enforce that choice. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that the chosen law will be applied unless there is no substantial relationship to the chosen state or if it contradicts a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. Since the altered text could lead to the application of a law with no substantial relationship to the agreement, it creates a legal contradiction as it goes against established principles of conflict of laws. While Scraped Law Snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation indicates that the altered text contradicts the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187, which may create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties understand and agree that no right or license under any patent, patent application, or know-how is granted to any other Party or any other person by this Agreement or by any disclosure of any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties understand and agree that a limited, non-exclusive, royalty-free license under any patent, patent application, or know-how is granted to any other Party or any other person by this Agreement or by any disclosure of any CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing an automatic, non-exclusive license could conflict with the Bayh-Dole Act if the research under the JRA is federally funded. The Bayh-Dole Act gives universities and small businesses the first right to elect title to inventions made with federal funding, and granting an automatic license could interfere with their ability to commercialize these inventions and comply with the Act's requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 202(c)(7) outlines requirements concerning licensing. If the invention was federally funded, the automatic license grant may contradict the obligations of the grantee or contractor under the Bayh-Dole Act, affecting their rights to the invention and ability to comply with federal regulations.

Disposition of rights (a) Each nonprofit organization or small business firm may, within a reasonable time after disclosure as required by paragraph (c)(1) of this section, elect to retain title to any subject invention: Provided, however , That a funding agreement may provide otherwise (i) when the contractor is not located in the United States or does not have a place of
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 'limited, non-exclusive, royalty-free license,' which directly contradicts the original text that stated 'no right or license' is granted. The Law Explanation points out that this automatic license grant may conflict with the obligations of the grantee or contractor under the Bayh-Dole Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 35 USC 202(a), states that a nonprofit organization or small business firm 'may...elect to retain title to any subject invention,' implying they have the right to choose, which is undermined by the automatic license introduced in the changed text.
